question: True or False? If every proper subsequence of ($x_n$) converges, then ($x_n$) converges as well. Answer True or False

conversation.content: True or False? If every proper subsequence of ($x_n$) converges, then ($x_n$) converges as well. Answer True or False

True.

If (x_n) did not converge it would have at least two distinct subsequential limits a ≠ b, hence there are subsequences (x_{n_k}) → a and (x_{m_k}) → b; interleaving them yields a proper subsequence that does not converge, contradicting the hypothesis. Therefore (x_n) converges.  
then the answer: True

True.

Suppose (x_n) does not converge; then there exist two subsequences (x_{n_k}) → a and (x_{m_k}) → b with a ≠ b. Choose these subsequences with disjoint index sets (possible by passing to tails). Interleave them to form a subsequence (x_{p_k}) = x_{n_1},x_{m_1},x_{n_2},x_{m_2},... which does not converge (it has two distinct cluster limits), and (x_{p_k}) is a proper subsequence of (x_n), contradicting the hypothesis. Hence (x_n) converges.  
then the answer: True

True.

Interleave them to form a proper subsequence x_{p_1},x_{p_2},… that has two cluster points a and b, so it does not converge—contradiction. Hence (x_n) converges.

True.

If (x_n) did not converge there exist two subsequences (x_{n_k})→a and (x_{m_k})→b with a≠b. Passing to tails we can ensure their index sets are disjoint, then interleave them to get a proper subsequence that has both limits a and b and thus does not converge, contradicting the hypothesis. True.

Assume (x_n) does not converge. Then there exist ε>0 and two disjoint infinite sets of indices producing subsequences with distinct limits a ≠ b (standard Bolzano–Weierstrass argument: nonconvergence gives at least two cluster points). Choose subsequences (x_{n_k})→a and (x_{m_k})→b with disjoint index sets; interleave them to form a proper subsequence x_{p_1}=x_{n_1}, x_{p_2}=x_{m_1}, x_{p_3}=x_{n_2},… which has both limit points a and b and therefore does not converge, contradicting the hypothesis. Hence (x_n) converges.  
then the answer: True

True.

If every proper subsequence converges, then in particular every tail (sequence obtained by removing finitely many initial terms) converges. Any two tails are proper subsequences, so they must have the same limit L (otherwise interleaving two tails with different limits would produce a proper subsequence that fails to converge). Since for each ε>0 some tail lies within ε of L, the original sequence converges to L.  
then the answer: True
